title: Geographic Patterns of Eviction
text:
Evictions don’t happen uniformly across a city. This report uses geocoded court records to visualize where granted evictions concentrate in Tulsa County. Each data point represents a court case where eviction was granted and the filing address was geocoded with high confidence (accuracy ≥ 0.85).
The goal is not to explain why evictions cluster — that requires socioeconomic context this dataset doesn’t contain. The goal is to surface where they cluster so that further investigation can focus on the right areas.

title: Geographic Patterns of Eviction
text:
5 Key Observations
The density maps show whether eviction filings cluster in specific neighborhoods or distribute broadly. When clusters are stable across years, they often correspond to areas with high concentrations of rental housing, lower-income demographics, or specific property management patterns.
The geocoding quality table confirms that the vast majority of records have exact or near-exact location data, meaning the spatial patterns are reliable rather than artifacts of geocoding errors.

Data pipeline: targets::tar_make() → data/parquet/ → this report.

title: R Analytics Platform Learning Project
text:
This is an educational R project that combines lessons from several internal projects into a single, integrated learning environment. It demonstrates:

Pipeline orchestration with targets — caching, reproducibility, dependency graphs
Modern data formats — Parquet files queried with Arrow and DuckDB
Reproducible environments — renv for R packages, Docker for system dependencies
Automated publishing — Quarto website deployed via GitHub Actions to GitHub Pages
Cloud storage — GCS-backed artifact persistence with Workload Identity Federation

title: R Analytics Platform Learning Project
text:
2 Reports

2.1 Eviction Trends

Year-over-year analysis of eviction filings in Tulsa County (2021–2025), exploring how volume has changed over time and what the trajectory suggests.



2.2 Geographic Patterns

Spatial analysis of where eviction filings concentrate geographically, using geocoded court records to identify hotspot areas.


Built as a learning project. See docs/JOURNEY.md for the full build log.

title: Eviction Trends in Tulsa County
text: This report tracks the volume of granted eviction filings in Tulsa County from 2021 through 2025. “Granted” means a court issued a judgment in favor of eviction — not every filing results in one. The data comes from Oklahoma court records, geocoded and filtered to cases with high-confidence location data (accuracy ≥ 0.85).

title: Eviction Trends in Tulsa County
text:
4 What the Data Suggests
The trend line tells a story about housing stability in Tulsa County. Changes in eviction volume can reflect shifts in housing policy, economic conditions, pandemic-era protections expiring, or changes in landlord behavior.

Data pipeline: targets::tar_make() → data/parquet/ → this report.
